steps.title: Identify what Hensley et al. were investigating
steps.content:
The passage says the researchers worked “To determine if perceived category similarity predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions,” and collected two key measurements for each brand pair—consumers' similarity ratings and calculated probability changes in purchasing.

For this hypothesis to be supported, these two measurements must be connected: higher similarity ratings should correspond to higher probability changes.

steps.title: Determine which finding supports the hypothesis
steps.content:
The correct answer reports a strong positive correlation between consumers' similarity ratings and the calculated probability changes.

This directly demonstrates that perceived category similarity predicts purchasing likelihood: as ratings increase, so do probability changes.

The other choices either introduce confounding variables, show no relationship between ratings and probabilities, or focus on unrelated purchasing patterns.

That relationship answers their core research question. B is correct.
